reviews.title: Lush, immersive fantasy
reviews.body: The worldbuilding in this book is incredibly rich without ever feeling overwhelming. I especially loved the way the magic tied into the emotional arcs.

reviews.title: Couldn’t put it down
reviews.body: Great pacing, memorable characters, and a finale that actually sticks the landing. A few slow spots in the middle, but overall a fantastic read.

reviews.title: Character-driven space mystery
reviews.body: This blended sci-fi and mystery in a really satisfying way. The found-family crew dynamic made the twists hit even harder.

reviews.title: Brilliant short cyberpunk tale
reviews.body: Tight, fast, and emotionally resonant. In under 100 pages it did more than many full-length novels, and the ending was perfect.

reviews.title: Heartfelt and hopeful
reviews.body: A sweet coming-of-age story with realistic friendships and just enough romance. The stargazing scenes were my favorite.

reviews.title: Smart, twisty sci‑fi
reviews.body: Great for fans who like big ideas mixed with human drama. The nonlinear structure took a chapter to adjust to but paid off in the end.

books.title: Echoes of Starlight
books.subtitle: A Luminous YA Fantasy
books.description: When a comet appears over the island city of Lyria, seventeen-year-old Mara discovers she can hear the stars sing—and that their song is fading. To save her world, she must unravel an ancient prophecy and confront a forgotten betrayal.

books.title: Shadows in Orbit
books.subtitle: A Starship Mystery
books.description: On the fringes of colonized space, the research vessel Aurora discovers a derelict ship with no life signs and a black box that has been deliberately wiped. As engineer-turned-sleuth Lyra investigates, she uncovers a conspiracy that could rewrite humanity’s place among the stars.

books.title: Whispers in Code
books.subtitle: A Cyberpunk Novella
books.description: In a neon-drenched megacity where memories can be edited like software, a data diver hears a voice in the code begging to be set free. To uncover the truth, she must break the law, hack her own past, and decide what it means to be real.

books.title: Summer of Stars
books.subtitle: A Young Adult Romance
books.description: When city girl Lina is shipped off to her grandparents’ rural observatory for the summer, she expects boredom. Instead she finds a sky full of secrets, a rival-turned-friend, and a choice that will change her future forever.

books.title: Fractured Orbits
books.subtitle: A Time-Bending Space Epic
books.description: A pilot caught in a temporal anomaly relives the same doomed mission across parallel timelines. Each loop reveals new allies, enemies, and pieces of a puzzle that stretches from deep space to the heart of her own past.

books.title: The Moonlit Citadel
books.subtitle: Book One of the Silver Veil Saga
books.description: An exiled scholar returns to the floating citadel of her childhood to stop a usurper king from tearing the veil between worlds—and discovers that the magic she fears most is the only thing that can save them.

books.title: The Ember Crown
books.subtitle: A Rebellion Ignites
books.description: Bound to a cursed crown that burns away lies, a reluctant heir must decide whether to expose her family’s crimes to the world or watch her kingdom fall into war.

books.title: Threads of the Dreaming Sky
books.subtitle: A Journey Between Worlds
books.description: Every night, dreamweaver Kairo stitches together the sky above his desert city. When the constellations begin to fray, he must cross into the realm of dreams itself to repair the damage before reality unravels.

books.title: Gates of the Drowned Realm
books.subtitle: The Silver Veil Saga Book Two
books.description: As the ocean rises to claim the lowlands, ancient gates emerge from the depths. To stop an invasion from the drowned realm, a band of unlikely allies must venture beneath the waves—and bargain with the dead.

books.title: Crimson Oath
books.subtitle: A Tale of Blood and Honor
books.description: A disgraced knight swears a forbidden oath to protect the enemy princess, binding their fates as war consumes the continent.

books.title: The Silent Map
books.subtitle: A Cartographer Mystery
books.description: When a reclusive mapmaker is found dead in his workshop, his apprentice discovers a city map that shows streets which don’t exist—yet. Each vanished alley may be a clue to the killer’s next move.

books.title: The Painted Key
books.subtitle: Art, Secrets, and Lies
books.description: An art restorer discovers a key hidden beneath layers of paint in a centuries-old portrait, drawing her into a cold case involving a missing girl and a stolen masterpiece.

books.title: The Clockwork Ward
books.subtitle: A Hospital Thriller
books.description: In a cutting-edge hospital wing where every heartbeat is tracked by experimental AI, patients begin to die in impossible ways. A night nurse must decide whether the system is failing—or killing on purpose.

books.title: Last Door on the Left
books.subtitle: A Locked-Corridor Mystery
books.description: A true-crime podcaster checks into a crumbling seaside hotel to investigate a decades-old disappearance. The manager warns her never to open the last door on the left. Naturally, she does.

books.title: The Last Starship Home
books.subtitle: A Novella of Return
books.description: After centuries in cryosleep, a generation ship finally approaches its destination—only to find a message waiting from Earth. One officer must decide whether to turn back or forge ahead into the unknown.

books.title: City of Glass and Smoke
books.subtitle: A Dystopian YA Adventure
books.description: In a city where every citizen’s future is determined by a single test at sixteen, a girl who fails the exam discovers a hidden underground of other ‘failures’ planning to bring the system down.

blog_posts.title: Finding Your Story’s North Star
blog_posts.excerpt: How to choose a guiding question for your draft so you stop wandering in the middle and start writing with intention.
blog_posts.content:
Every novel I write begins with a question I don’t know how to answer yet. Not a plot twist, not a theme statement, but a question. For ECHOES OF STARLIGHT, it was: What would you sacrifice if you knew the world would never remember you did it?

In this post, we’ll walk through a simple process for identifying your story’s “North Star” question, testing whether it’s specific enough to shape scenes, and using it to make decisions when you’re stuck in the mushy middle.

1. Start with the emotion you want the reader to feel at the end.
2. Turn that emotion into a question your protagonist can’t easily answer.
3. Check that at least three major scenes in your outline put pressure on that question.
4. Write the question on a sticky note and tape it to your monitor.

Whenever you’re unsure what happens next, ask: which choice will put more pressure on this question? That’s usually the more interesting option.

blog_posts.title: Drafting vs Revising: Switching Brain Modes
blog_posts.excerpt: Your drafting brain and your revising brain want completely different things. Here’s how to stop making them fight.
blog_posts.content:
Writers often describe drafting as discovery and revising as problem-solving—and then try to do both at once. No wonder it feels impossible.

In this article, I break the process into two distinct brain modes:

**Drafting Mode**
- Your only job is to generate material.
- You’re allowed to be messy, repetitive, and clichéd.
- You measure success in words written, not quality.

**Revising Mode**
- Your job is to make decisions.
- You question every scene’s purpose.
- You measure success in clarity and impact.

We’ll talk about concrete rituals you can use to cue each mode (different playlists, locations, or even fonts), and I’ll share the three questions I ask before I allow myself to start revising any new project.

blog_posts.title: Building a Daily Writing Habit That Survives Real Life
blog_posts.excerpt: Perfectionism destroys more writing routines than laziness ever will. Let’s design a habit that bends without breaking.
blog_posts.content:
Most writing advice assumes you have unlimited time, quiet, and energy. I don’t—and I’m guessing you don’t either.

In this post, we’ll design a flexible daily habit using three pillars:

1. **Minimum viable session**: the tiniest unit of progress that still counts.
2. **Triggers and anchors**: habits that happen right before and after writing.
3. **Failure planning**: what you’ll do on the days everything falls apart.

I’ll share examples from my own schedule during book tours, day-job crunches, and family emergencies, plus a worksheet you can adapt to your life right now—not your imaginary perfect writing life five years from now.

blog_posts.title: Behind the Book: Writing Echoes of Starlight
blog_posts.excerpt: From abandoned drafts and wrong protagonists to the final comet-lit version that ended up on shelves.
blog_posts.content:
ECHOES OF STARLIGHT took eight years, four main characters, and one very stubborn comet to get right.

In this behind-the-scenes essay, I walk through:

- The original idea (a sci-fi heist!) and why it failed.
- How switching to a YA fantasy lens unlocked the emotional core.
- The structural decision that finally made the ending work.
- What I cut in the last month before copyedits.

You’ll also see side-by-side comparisons of early paragraphs and their final versions, with notes about what changed and why.

blog_posts.title: Worldbuilding Without Infodumps
blog_posts.excerpt: Readers want to live in your world, not listen to you lecture about it. Here are five techniques I rely on.
blog_posts.content:
We’ve all hit that chapter: three pages of lore before anything actually happens. As a reader, you skim. As a writer, you cringe.

In this craft-focused article, we’ll look at five ways to weave worldbuilding details into active scenes:

1. **Consequences first, explanations later.**
2. **Use character opinions instead of neutral description.**
3. **Let setting details create problems, not just vibes.**
4. **Borrow mystery techniques to reveal information in stages.**
5. **Track reader knowledge with a simple scene-by-scene grid.**

I’ll annotate a scene from THE MOONLIT CITADEL to show exactly where and how setting details are doing double duty.

blog_posts.title: How I Outline a Mystery Novel
blog_posts.excerpt: Outlining a mystery means plotting both the crime and the experience of discovering it. I use two timelines to keep them straight.
blog_posts.content:
Mystery readers are delightfully cruel: they want to be fooled, but they also want to be able to say they *could* have solved it.

To balance fairness with surprise, I outline my mysteries on two parallel tracks:

- **The story of the crime**, in strict chronological order.
- **The story of the investigation**, which is how the reader experiences events.

In this post, I share a downloadable template, then walk through how I used it for THE SILENT MAP, including where I planted red herrings, how I handled reveals, and the one clue I moved three times during revisions.

blog_posts.title: Launch Day Reflections: Shadows in Orbit
blog_posts.excerpt: A few thoughts on writing hopeful space opera in a not-always-hopeful world.
blog_posts.content:
SHADOWS IN ORBIT is my love letter to found families, messy hope, and the weird corners of space that never make it into shiny recruitment posters.

In this more personal post, I talk about:

- The real-life news stories that shaped the book.
- Why I chose to center engineers and maintenance crew instead of captains.
- The reader emails that meant the most to me during revisions.
- What I’m carrying forward into the sequel.

No major spoilers, but I do reference a few character arcs, so you may want to read the book first.

blog_posts.title: Creating Compelling Side Characters
blog_posts.excerpt: If your side characters only exist to deliver information or die for the plot, your story is missing some of its heart.
blog_posts.content:
Side characters are the mirrors that show us who the protagonist really is. When I’m developing them, I ask three questions:

1. What does this character want that has nothing to do with the protagonist?
2. How does their presence make the protagonists choices harder (or clearer)?
3. What tiny detail would make a reader recognize them instantly?

In this post, I break down examples from several of my books, including a side character in THE CLOCKWORK WARD who almost took over the entire novel. You’ll also find a checklist you can run through for every recurring character in your cast.

blog_posts.title: What I Learned from Ten Years of Rejection
blog_posts.excerpt: I collected over 200 rejections before signing with an agent. Here are the lessons Id keep—even if I could skip the pain.
blog_posts.content:
Before my first book deal, I wrote four trunk novels, cried over a spreadsheet full of regrettably, we must pass, and almost quit twice.

This essay isnt about craft; its about survival. I talk about managing expectations, finding community, setting boundaries with feedback, and the one mindset shift that finally made the process sustainable.

If youre in the query trenches—or deep in submission with no news—this is for you.

blog_posts.title: NaNoWriMo 2025 Survival Guide
blog_posts.excerpt: Fifty thousand words in thirty days doesnt require magic. It requires planning for your future tired self.
blog_posts.content:
Every November I draft something messy and overcaffeinated alongside thousands of other writers. Over the years, Ive learned that the difference between a joyful NaNoWriMo and a miserable one usually comes down to logistics.

In this guide, well map out:

- A realistic writing schedule around your actual life.
- A menu of five-minute wins for the days youre exhausted.
- A list of rewards that arent all sugar or money.

Ill also share the loose scene list Ill be using for my next project, plus a printable tracker you can tape to your wall.

blog_posts.title: A Scene-by-Scene Revision Checklist
blog_posts.excerpt: Big-picture edits are important, but most novels are saved (or sunk) one scene at a time.
blog_posts.content:
When I get an edit letter, my first instinct is to hide under a blanket. Once Im done with that, I break the work down into a scene-by-scene checklist.

In this article, I share the exact questions I ask about every scene in a second draft:

- Does this scene change something that cant be easily undone?
- Is the conflict internal, external, or both?
- What new information does the reader learn—and what new question do they leave with?

Youll find a downloadable worksheet and a worked example using a chapter from GATES OF THE DROWNED REALM. This is a great companion to my earlier post on drafting vs revising brain modes.

blog_posts.title: Layering Subplots Without Losing Your Main Story
blog_posts.excerpt: Subplots should make your main story sharper, not muddier. Here’s how I decide which ones to keep, cut, or combine.
blog_posts.content:
At some point in drafting SHADOWS IN ORBIT, I realized I had written six different subplots. Three of them were doing real work. The others were carefully written noise.

In this deep-dive craft post, we’ll:

1. Define the three jobs a subplot can do: **mirror**, **foil**, or **pressure**.
2. Map each subplot to the moment it collides with the main plot.
3. Use a color-coding method to spot which threads quietly disappear.
4. Practice combining two weak subplots into one stronger, cleaner arc.

I include a downloadable worksheet and a marked-up example from my work-in-progress so you can see the method in action.

comments.body: I used this checklist on a messy second draft chapter tonight and immediately spotted two scenes that weren’t actually changing anything. Cut them and the pacing already feels better.
That’s awesome to hear! The “does this scene change something?” question is usually the one that hurts the most, but it’s also the most clarifying.
Saved this and printed the worksheet. I’ve always struggled with revisions feeling vague—having concrete questions for each scene makes it feel doable.
The example from GATES OF THE DROWNED REALM was super helpful. Seeing the before/after made it clear how much tightening happens at the scene level.
Quick question: do you use this checklist on the very first revision pass, or only after big structural changes are done?
Great question—usually I do one high-level pass first (moving and cutting scenes), then run this checklist on what’s left so I’m not polishing scenes that will disappear.
The mirror/foil/pressure framework clicked for me immediately. Realized one subplot in my WIP wasn’t doing any of those jobs—it’s gone now.
Loved the color-coding idea. I marked mine up and could literally see which threads vanished for 100+ pages.
The “North Star question” helped me realize my current draft doesn’t actually have one yet—no wonder the middle keeps wandering.
I wrote my North Star on a sticky note like you suggested and stuck it to my laptop. It’s already changed a couple of scene decisions.
Do you ever discover a different North Star halfway through and switch, or do you try to stay loyal to the first one?
Absolutely—sometimes the original question was really a warm-up. If a better, more electric question shows up mid-draft, I’ll adjust the book toward that.
I tried changing fonts between drafting and revising like you mentioned and it weirdly helped my brain switch gears.
The idea of measuring drafting days in words and revising days in decisions made was a lightbulb moment for me.
Bookmarking this for November. The five-minute wins list is exactly what I need for days when I get home from work exhausted.
I always overestimate future-me’s energy. Planning rewards ahead of time might actually keep me going past week two.
Reading this while sitting on a fresh rejection email. It hurt, but your mindset shift section gave me something concrete to hold onto.
Thank you for not skipping over the messy parts. It helps to know books I love came from years of nods.
Finished SHADOWS IN ORBIT last night and I already miss the crew. Loved hearing about the real-world inspirations here.
